findings.description: Email is interpolated into the SQL query via an f-string, enabling SQL injection and authentication bypass.
Session expiry check is inverted: live sessions (expires_at in the future) are deleted and rejected while expired sessions fall through and are accepted at line 109.
As a consequence of the inverted check, an expired session token is treated as valid and returns the user_id, defeating session expiration.
find_user_by_email loads the entire users table with SELECT * and scans it in Python instead of using a WHERE email = ? query, giving O(n) cost per lookup.
rotate_token relies on the broken validate_session, so it only succeeds for already-expired sessions and fails for live ones (cascading failure).
LOCKOUT_MINUTES is defined but never used; lockout has no time window, so an account stays locked permanently once failed_attempts reaches the max with no automatic expiry.
Unknown-email path returns early without performing a dummy password hash, making user enumeration possible via response-time differences versus the wrong-password path.
Sessions are stored only in an in-process dict, so they are lost on restart, not shared across workers, and grow unbounded unless cleanup_expired is called manually.
PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 uses 100,000 iterations, below current OWASP guidance (~600,000) for this algorithm.
datetime.utcnow() is deprecated in Python 3.12+ and returns naive datetimes; prefer datetime.now(timezone.utc).
find_user_by_email does not filter on is_active whereas login does, an inconsistency that may return inactive users where callers do not expect them.
A locked account returns None identically to a bad-password result, which avoids enumeration but also hides lockout state from legitimate callers (minor, possibly intentional).
Mixed typing styles: typing.Optional is imported while builtin generics like dict[str, Session] are used; standardize on one.
SELECT * couples the code to table column order/names; selecting explicit columns is more robust.
